title: Why We Built Copilot Mode: Human Receptionists + AI, Working Together
summary: Most AI phone systems make you choose: human or AI. Radar's copilot mode gives you both. Here's how it works and why we built it.
context: Deep dive into Radar's copilot mode. The problem: some businesses love what AI can do (scheduling, CRM sync, dispatch) but want a human on the phone for the personal touch. Competitors force an either/or choice. Copilot mode puts the human receptionist on the call while Radar works behind the scenes — extracting caller info in real time with confidence scores, surfacing knowledge base answers when callers ask questions, suggesting follow-up questions when data is missing, looking up caller history, and queuing up all integrations (calendar booking, ServiceTitan job creation, Discord alerts, SMS to on-call tech). After the call, the receptionist reviews everything on a dashboard, edits anything the AI got wrong, enables/disables specific integrations, and clicks 'Confirm & Execute' — one click fires everything. No manual data entry, no forgetting to create the job, no sticky notes. Copilot mode costs half the price of full AI mode — a great entry point for businesses not ready to go full AI. And it dramatically boosts receptionist productivity: no more manual data entry, no more forgetting to create the job, no more post-call paperwork. Your receptionist can handle twice the call volume because Radar does all the backend work. This is a massive differentiator against Smith.ai, Ruby, AnswerConnect, and every AI-only platform. Position it as: your receptionist brings the relationship, Radar brings the automation. Link to /radar.

title: Smith.ai vs Radar: Which AI Receptionist Is Better for Plumbers?

Radar actually books jobs, dispatches emergencies to on-call techs, creates ServiceTitan/Jobber entries, and handles 30+ languages. Radar is custom-trained on each business — not a script reader. Price comparison: Radar starts at $149/mo flat vs Smith.ai's per-call pricing that adds up fast for busy plumbers. Name Smith.ai in headings for SEO.

title: Why Traditional Answering Services Don't Work for Tradespeople
summary: You're paying $300/month for someone to take a message and email it to you. Here's why that model is broken — and what actually works.
context: Tear down the traditional answering service model (AnswerConnect, PATLive, MAP Communications): they charge per minute, use scripts, can't access your calendar or CRM, can't dispatch, and create a game of phone tag. Every message is a delay. For a plumber with a burst pipe call at midnight, 'we'll pass along the message' means a lost customer. Compare this to Radar: AI answers instantly, books the job, dispatches the on-call tech, and alerts the owner — all before the caller hangs up. The message-taking model was built for offices, not field service. Position Radar as the modern replacement.

title: Zapier vs n8n: Which Is Better for Service Business Automation?
summary: Zapier is easier. n8n is more powerful. Here's which one makes sense for your service business — and when to skip both.
context: Compare Zapier and n8n specifically for service business use cases: call routing, job creation, emergency alerts, calendar booking, CRM syncing. Zapier is great for simple workflows but gets expensive fast ($50-100+/mo) and has limits on complex logic. n8n is self-hosted, free, and handles complex branching — but requires technical setup. The real angle: for most tradespeople, you shouldn't be building automations at all. Radar and Taskline handle the complex stuff out of the box. Position Solvr Labs as the done-for-you alternative to DIY automation.
